sections.paragraph:
ὑψηλόφθαλμος, ον (hapax leg.—ὑψηλοὶ ὀφθαλμοί in the lit. sense Physiogn. I 327, 2) lifting up the eyes, perh. in pride, though the context calls rather for in lust or wantonness D 3:3 (s. Wengst, Didache p. 71 n. 16, w. ref. to TestIss 7:2; 1QS 1:6; CD 2:16; 2 Pt 2:14; v.l. in Apost. Const. 7 ῥιψόφθαλμος).—DELG s.v. ὕψι.

hapax hapax leg. = hapax legomenon (occurring only once)

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

Physiogn. Physiogn. = Scriptores Physiognomici, various dates—List 5

perh. perh. = perhaps

D D = Didache, except that in a list of manuscripts or as textual variant D refers to Codex Bezae—List 1

ref. ref. = reference(s)

TestIss TestIss = Testament of Issachar, s. Test12Patr—List 2

1QS 1QS = The Manual of Discipline—List 2, end

CD CD = Cairo (Genizah text of the) Damascus (Document), a.k.a. Zadokite Document or Damascus Rule—List 2, end

v.l. v.l. = varia lectio (variant reading)

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 1045
